questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
(a) If $$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}x^2, & \text { if } x \geq 1 \\ x, & \text { if } x<1\end{array}\right.$$, then show that $$f$$ is not differentiable at $$x=1$$.

OR

(b) Find the value(s) of '$$\lambda$$', if the function

$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda x}{x^2}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array} \text { is continuous at } x=0 .\right.$$
questions.solution:
$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} x^2, & \text { if } x \geq 1 \\ x, & \text { if } x<1 \end{array}\right.$$

$$f(x)$$ is defined at $$x=1$$ and $$f(1)=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} f_{-}^{\prime}(1) & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h} \\ & =\frac{1+h-1}{h}=1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} f_{+}^{\prime}(1) & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1+h)^2-1}{h} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h(2+h)}{h} \\ f_{-}^{\prime}(1) & \neq f_{+}^{\prime}(1) \end{aligned}$$

Hence $$f(x)$$ is not differentiable at $$x=1$$

OR

$$\text { (b) } f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda x}{x^2}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array} \text { is continuous at } x=0 .\right.$$

$$\begin{aligned} f(0) & =1 \\ \substack{f(x) \\ x \rightarrow 0^{-}} & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda(0-h)}{(0-h)^2} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda h}{h^2} \\ & =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \lambda^2\left(\frac{\sin \lambda h}{\lambda h}\right)^2 \\ & =\lambda^2 \times 1=\lambda^2 \\ f(0) & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x) \\ 1 & =\lambda^2 \therefore \lambda= \pm 1 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If the range of the function $ f(x) = \frac{5-x}{x^2 - 3x + 2} , \ x \neq 1, 2, $ is $ (-\infty , \alpha] \cup [\beta, \infty) $, then $ \alpha^2 + \beta^2 $ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=\frac{5-x}{x^2-3 x+2} \\ & y x^2-3 x y+2 y+x-5=0 \\ & y z^2+(-3 y+1) x+(2 y-5)=0 \end{aligned}$$

Case I : If $y=0$ (Accepted)

$$\Rightarrow x=5$$

Case II : If $y \neq 0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{D} \geq 0 \\ & (-3 y+1)^2-4(y)(2 y-5) \geq 0 \\ & 9 y^2+1-6 y-8 y^2+20 y \geq 0 \\ & y^2+14 y+1 \geq 0 \\ & (y+7)^2-48 \geq 0 \\ & |y+7| \geq 4 \sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow y+7 \geq 4 \sqrt{3} \text { or } \mathrm{y}+7 \leq-4 \sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{y} \geq 4 \sqrt{3}-7 \text { or } \mathrm{y} \leq-4 \sqrt{3}-7 \end{aligned}$$

From Case I and Case II

$$y \in(-\infty,-4 \sqrt{3}-7] \cup[4 \sqrt{3}-7, \infty)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { So } \alpha=-4 \sqrt{3}-7 \\ & \quad \beta=4 \sqrt{3}-7 \\ & \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow a^2+b^2 & =(-4 \sqrt{3}-7)^2+(4 \sqrt{3}-7)^2 \\ & =2(48+49) \\ & =194 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let f(x) and g(x) be two real polynomials of degree 2 and 1 respectively. If $$f(g(x)) = 8{x^2} - 2x$$ and $$g(f(x)) = 4{x^2} + 6x + 1$$, then the value of $$f(2) + g(2)$$ is _________.
questions.solution:
$f(g(x))=8 x^{2}-2 x$ $$ g(f(x))=4 x^{2}+6 x+1 $$

let $f(x)=c x^{2}+d x+e$

$g(x)=a x+b$

$f(g(x))=c(a x+b)^{2}+d(a x+b)+e \equiv 8 x^{2}-2 x$

$g(f(x))=a\left(c x^{2}+d x+e\right)+b \equiv 4 x^{2}+6 x+1$

$\therefore \quad a c=4 \quad a d=6 \quad a e+b=1$

$a^{2} c=8 \quad 2 a b c+a d=-2 \quad c b^{2}+b d+e=0$

By solving

$a=2 \quad b=-1$

$c=2 \quad d=3 \quad e=1$

$\therefore \quad f(x)=2 x^{2}+3 x+1$

$g(x)=2 x-1$

$f(2)+g(2)=2(2)^{2}+3(2)+1+2(2)-1$

$=18$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $f(x)=\log _{\mathrm{e}} x$ and $g(x)=\frac{x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2}{2 x^2-2 x+1}$. Then the domain of $f \circ g$ is
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\ln x \\ & g(x)=\frac{x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2}{2 x^2-2 x+1} \\ & D_g \in R \\ & D_f \in(0, \infty) \end{aligned}$$

For $D_{f o g} \Rightarrow g(x)>0$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2}{2 x^2-2 x+1}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow x^4-2 x^3+3 x^2-2 x+2>0 \end{aligned}$$

Clearly $\mathrm{x}<0$ satisfies which are included in option (1) only.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let $x=0$. Then the given relation

$ f(xy)=f(x+y) $

becomes

$ f(0\cdot y)=f(0+y)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(0)=f(y)\quad\text{for all real }y. $

This shows $f$ is a constant function.

Since $f$ is constant and $f(5)=10$, it follows that for every input, including $0$,

$ f(0)=10. $

Answer: Option D (10).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The domain of the function $$f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{[x]^{2}-3[x]-10}}$$ is : ( where $$[\mathrm{x}]$$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $$x$$ )

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If f(x) = x<sup>2</sup> + 2 and g(x) = 2x - 3, then what is (fog)(1) equal to ?
questions.solution:
To find $(f \circ g)(1)$, we need to evaluate the function $f$ at the point $g(1)$. Let's break this down step by step.

First, we need to find the value of $g(1)$:

Given $g(x) = 2x - 3$, we can plug in $x = 1$:

$$ g(1) = 2(1) - 3 = 2 - 3 = -1 $$

Now that we have $g(1) = -1$, we need to evaluate $f(-1)$:

Given $f(x) = x^2 + 2$, we can plug in $x = -1$:

$$ f(-1) = (-1)^2 + 2 = 1 + 2 = 3 $$

Therefore, $(f \circ g)(1) = f(g(1)) = f(-1) = 3$.

So, the correct option is A:

3

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
From the given relation

$$f\biggl(\frac{x}{y}\biggr)=\frac{f(x)}{f(y)}$$

set $x=y$. Then

$$f(1)=\frac{f(y)}{f(y)}=1.$$

To show multiplicativity, let $a=xy$ and $b=y$. Then $\frac{a}{b}=\frac{xy}{y}=x$, so

$$f(x)=f\Bigl(\frac{a}{b}\Bigr)=\frac{f(a)}{f(b)} \;\Longrightarrow\;f(a)=f(b)\,f(x)\,. $$

That is, for all positive $x,y$,

$$f(xy)=f(x)\,f(y)\,. $$

Now, since $f(2)=3$, we get

$$f(4)=f(2\cdot2)=f(2)\,f(2)=3\cdot3=9.$$

Finally,

$$f(1)\,f(4)=1\cdot9=9.$$

Answer: Option C (9).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
For $$x \in R - \left\{ {0,1} \right\}$$, Let f1(x) = $$1\over x$$, f2 (x) = 1 – x

and f3 (x) = $$1 \over {1 - x}$$ be three given

functions. If a function, J(x) satisfies

(f2 o J o f1) (x) = f3 (x) then J(x) is equal to :
questions.solution:
Given,

f1(x) = $${1 \over x}$$

f2(x) = 1 $$-$$ x

f3(x) = $${1 \over {1 - x}}$$

(f2 $$ \cdot $$ J $$ \cdot $$ f1) (x) = f3(x)

$$ \Rightarrow $$   f2 {J(f1(x))} = f3(x)

$$ \Rightarrow $$   f2{J ($${1 \over x}$$)} = $${1 \over {1 - x}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$   1 $$-$$ J($${1 \over x}$$) = $${1 \over {1 - x}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$  J($${{1 \over x}}$$) = 1 $$-$$ $${{1 \over {1 - x}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$   J ($${{1 \over { x}}}$$) = $${{ - x} \over {1 - x}}$$ = $${x \over {x - 1}}$$

Put x inplace of $${1 \over x}$$

$$ \therefore $$   J(x) = $${{{1 \over x}} \over {{1 \over x} - 1}}$$

= $${1 \over {1 - x}} = {f_3}\left( x \right)$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: A function satisfies $f(x-y)=\frac{f(x)}{f(y)}$, where f(y) ≠ 0. If f(1) = 0.5, then what is f(2) + f(3) + f(4) + f(5) + f(6) equal to ?
questions.solution:
We are given:

$ f(x-y) = \frac{f(x)}{f(y)}, \quad f(y) \neq 0, \quad f(1) = \tfrac{1}{2} $

We are asked to find:

$ f(2) + f(3) + f(4) + f(5) + f(6) $

Step 1. Put $x = y$.

$ f(x-x) = f(0) = \frac{f(x)}{f(x)} = 1 $

So,

$ f(0) = 1 $

Step 2. Put $y = 0$.

$ f(x-0) = f(x) = \frac{f(x)}{f(0)} = \frac{f(x)}{1} $

This is consistent.

Step 3. Try $x=2, y=1$.

$ f(2-1) = f(1) = \frac{f(2)}{f(1)} $

$ f(1) = \frac{f(2)}{f(1)} \implies f(2) = f(1)^2 $

Since $f(1) = \tfrac{1}{2}$,

$ f(2) = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \tfrac{1}{4} $

Step 4. Similarly, put $x=3, y=1$.

$ f(3-1) = f(2) = \frac{f(3)}{f(1)} $

$ f(3) = f(2) f(1) = \tfrac{1}{4}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{1}{8} $

Step 5. Next, for $x=4, y=1$.

$ f(4-1) = f(3) = \frac{f(4)}{f(1)} $

$ f(4) = f(3) f(1) = \tfrac{1}{8}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{1}{16} $

Step 6. For $x=5, y=1$:

$ f(5-1) = f(4) = \frac{f(5)}{f(1)} $

$ f(5) = f(4) f(1) = \tfrac{1}{16}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{1}{32} $

Step 7. For $x=6, y=1$:

$ f(6-1) = f(5) = \frac{f(6)}{f(1)} $

$ f(6) = f(5) f(1) = \tfrac{1}{32}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{1}{64} $

Step 8. Add all required values.

$ f(2) + f(3) + f(4) + f(5) + f(6) = \tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{1}{8} + \tfrac{1}{16} + \tfrac{1}{32} + \tfrac{1}{64} $

Take LCM as 64:

$ = \frac{16}{64} + \frac{8}{64} + \frac{4}{64} + \frac{2}{64} + \frac{1}{64} $

$ = \frac{31}{64} $

Final Answer:

$ \boxed{\tfrac{31}{64}} $

So, the correct option is D.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
If the functions are defined as $$f(x) = \sqrt x $$ and $$g(x) = \sqrt {1 - x} $$, then what is the common domain of the following functions :

f + g, f $$-$$ g, f/g, g/f, g $$-$$ f where $$(f \pm g)(x) = f(x) \pm g(x),(f/g)x = {{f(x)} \over {g(x)}}$$
questions.solution:
$$f + g = \sqrt x + \sqrt {1 - x} $$

$$ \Rightarrow x \ge 0$$ & $$1 - x \ge 0 \Rightarrow x \in [0,1]$$

$$f - g = \sqrt x - \sqrt {1 - x} $$

$$ \Rightarrow x \ge 0$$ & $$1 - x \ge 0 \Rightarrow x \in [0,1]$$

$$f/g = {{\sqrt x } \over {\sqrt {1 - x} }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow x \ge 0$$ & $$1 - x > 0 \Rightarrow x \in [0,1)$$

$$g/f = {{\sqrt {1 - x} } \over {\sqrt x }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 - x \ge 0$$ & $$x > 0 \Rightarrow x \in (0,1]$$

$$g - f = \sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x $$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 - x \ge 0$$ & $$x \ge 0 \Rightarrow x \in [0,1]$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$x \in (0,1)$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $$f(x)=x^2-2 x+1$$ and $$f \circ g(x)=x^2+2 x+1$$, then $$g(x)$$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, $$f(x)=x^2-2 x+1$$ and

$$\begin{array}{ll} & f \circ g(x)=x^2+2 x+1 \\ \therefore & f(g(x))=x^2+2 x+1 \\ \Rightarrow & (g(x))^2-2\left(g(x)+1=x^2+2 x+1\right. \\ \Rightarrow & (g(x))^2-2\left(g(x)-x^2-2 x=0\right. \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=\frac{-(-2) \pm \sqrt{(-2)^2-4\left(-x^2-2 x\right)(1)}}{2(1)} \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4+4 x^2+8 x}}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=\frac{2 \pm 2 \sqrt{x^2+2 x+1}}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=1 \pm(x+1) \\ \Rightarrow & g(x)=x+2-x \end{array}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let $$f, g: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$$ be defined as :

$$f(x)=|x-1| \text { and } g(x)= \begin{cases}\mathrm{e}^x, & x \geq 0 \\ x+1, & x \leq 0 .\end{cases}$$

Then the function $$f(g(x))$$ is
options:
neither one-one nor onto.
one-one but not onto.
both one-one and onto.
onto but not one-one.
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)= \begin{cases}x-1, & x \geq 1 \\ 1-x & x<0\end{cases} \\ & g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} e^x & ; \quad x \geq 0 \\ x+1 & ; \quad x \leq 0 \end{array}\right. \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} f(g(x)) & = \begin{cases}g(x)-1, & g(x) \geq 1 \\ 1-g(x) & g(x)<1\end{cases} \\ & =\left\{\begin{array}{cl} e^x-1 & x \geq 0 \\ -x & x<0 \end{array}\right. \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If the domain of the function $$f(x)=\log _e\left(\frac{2 x+3}{4 x^2+x-3}\right)+\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x-1}{x+2}\right)$$ is $$(\alpha, \beta]$$, then the value of $$5 \beta-4 \alpha$$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{2 x+3}{4 x^2+x-3}>0 \text { and }-1 \leq \frac{2 x-1}{x+2} \leq 1 \\ & \frac{2 x+3}{(4 x-3)(x+1)}>0 \quad \frac{3 x+1}{x+2} \geq 0 \& \frac{x-3}{x+2} \leq 0 \end{aligned}$$

$$(-\infty,-2) \cup\left[\frac{-1}{3}, \infty\right)$$ ..... (1)

$$(-2,3]$$ ..... (2)

$$\left[\frac{-1}{3}, 3\right]$$ ..... (3) $$\quad (1) \cap(2) \cap(3)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{3}{4}, 3\right] \\ & \alpha=\frac{3}{4} \beta=3 \\ & 5 \beta-4 \alpha=15-3=12 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a function defined by $f(x)=(2+3 a) x^2+\left(\frac{a+2}{a-1}\right) x+b, a \neq 1$. If $f(x+y)=f(x)+f(\mathrm{y})+1-\frac{2}{7} x \mathrm{y}$, then the value of $28 \sum\limits_{i=1}^5|f(i)|$ is
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=(3 a+2) x^2+\left(\frac{a+2}{a-1}\right) x+b \\ & f\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)=f(x)+f(y)+1-\frac{2}{7} x y\quad\text{.... (1)} \end{aligned}$$

In (1) Put $x=y=0 \Rightarrow f(0)=2 f(0)+1 \Rightarrow f(0)=-1$

So, $\mathrm{f}(0)=0+0+\mathrm{b}=-1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=-1$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { In (1) Put } y=-x \Rightarrow f(0)=f(x)+f(-x)+1+\frac{2}{7} x^2 \\ & -1=2(3 a+2) x^2+2 b+1+\frac{2}{7} x^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & -1=\left(2(3 a+2)+\frac{2}{7}\right) x^2+1-2 \\ & \Rightarrow 6 a+4+\frac{2}{7}=0 \\ & a=-\frac{5}{7} \end{aligned}$$

So $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=-\frac{1}{7} \mathrm{x}^2-\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{x}-1$

$$\Rightarrow|\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})|=\frac{1}{28}\left|4 \mathrm{x}^2+21 \mathrm{x}+28\right|$$

Now, $28 \sum_{i=1}^5|f(i)|=28(|f(1)|+|f(2)|+\ldots+|f(5)|)$

$28 \cdot \frac{1}{28} \cdot 675=675$
